content_preview.title: 400,000-Year-Old Tooth Proteins Link Homo Erectus to Denisovans and Leave a Trace in Modern Humans
content_preview.summary: A Nature study extracts 11 proteins from six Homo erectus teeth found in China, revealing a shared amino acid variant with Denisovans now present in some Southeast Asian and Oceanian populations.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A team led by paleoanthropologist Qiaomei Fu of the Institute of Vertebrate Paleontology and Paleoanthropology at the Chinese Academy of Sciences has extracted proteins from six *Homo erectus* teeth unearthed at three archaeological sites in China, revealing a molecular signature shared with Denisovans that persists today in some living populations. The study, published in *Nature* on May 13, 2026, represents one of the first times ancient proteins have been used to trace potential ...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) returned dead link errors — verify URLs before resubmitting
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article covering a legitimate May 2026 Nature study on paleoproteomics. The four-section layout (What We Know, Why Enamel Proteins, What We Don't Know, Analysis) is clear and appropriately scoped. Tone is neutral and avoids sensationalism. The article correctly hedges the interbreeding hypothesis as probabilistic, not conclusive.
editor_notes.source_verification: Five of seven sources were verifiable from snapshots: source-2 (The Conversation, verified), source-3 (ScienceAlert, verified), source-4 (Scientific American, verified), source-5 (Live Science, verified), source-6 (Nature DOI, verified). Source-0 (phys.org) returned HTTP 403 and source-1 (archaeologymag.com) timed out — no snapshots available for those two. Key facts attributed to unavailable sources were independently confirmed against the Nature paper snapshot (source-6): Hexian and Sunjiadong sites, AMELY sex-determination technique, and AMBN-A253G/M273V variants all appear in the Nature paper. The John Hawks quote ('It's like, wow. The data just had to line up exactly right for this to happen.') was confirmed verbatim in the Scientific American snapshot. The AMBN-M273V frequency (21% Philippines, ~1% India) was confirmed in the Nature paper at 21% Philippines and 1.17% India — the article's 'around 1 percent' is acceptable rounding. The paper's phrase 'their shared habitats create opportunities for interactions' was confirmed verbatim in the ScienceAlert snapshot. One misattribution found: the article states 'Using micro-destructive acid etching to preserve the physical structure of the teeth... [as reported by Live Science]' but the Live Science snapshot (source-5) does not use the term 'micro-destructive acid etching' — it confirms only the extraction of 11 proteins and hundreds of amino acid positions. The Nature paper (source-6) uses 'acid etching method' for a localized small-area extraction, which substantively supports the description but does not use the 'micro-destructive' descriptor. This is a subordinate methodological detail misattributed to the wrong source; the underlying substance is accurate per the primary source. ScienceAlert and doi.org/Nature are reputable outlets not yet on the allowlist — both should be added.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All headline claims and lead facts are supported by the available sources. The core scientific narrative (11 proteins from 6 H. erectus teeth, AMBN-A253G unique to East Asian H. erectus, AMBN-M273V shared with Denisovans and present in ~21% of Filipinos, gene flow hypothesis) is accurate and well-sourced. H. erectus spread date (1.8 million years ago) and extinction estimate (~108,000 years ago) confirmed by Live Science. Sally Christine Reynolds affiliation (Associate Professor in Hominin Palaeoecology, Bournemouth University) confirmed by The Conversation. The 'braided river' metaphor attributed to Reynolds is confirmed by The Conversation snapshot.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No editorializing. The speculative elements (gene flow pathway, modern human inheritance) are correctly framed as hypotheses with appropriate hedging ('propose,' 'consistent with,' 'may have').
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate or substantially overlapping articles found in the archive. The April 2026 article on ancient DNA from Donghulin covers a different study, era, and methodology. The April article on mitochondrial DNA covers Australian settlement — no overlap.
concerns: Source-0 (phys.org) and source-1 (archaeologymag.com) snapshots unavailable; facts attributed to these sources were independently confirmed via the Nature paper snapshot.

The term 'micro-destructive acid etching' and its attribution to Live Science is not supported by the Live Science snapshot. The Nature paper uses 'acid etching method' for a localized extraction, which substantively aligns but lacks the 'micro-destructive' qualifier. This is a minor misattribution of a subordinate methodological descriptor.
recommendations: Add sciencealert.com, nature.com, and doi.org to config/source_allowlist.txt — all are reputable science sources.
A corrections note on the 'micro-destructive acid etching' misattribution to Live Science is warranted.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. The article is factually strong, well-structured, and covers a legitimate peer-reviewed Nature study accurately. The only editorial issue is a minor misattribution: the 'micro-destructive acid etching' description is attributed to Live Science, but that snapshot does not contain this term. The underlying fact is confirmed by the Nature paper. A corrections note documenting this misattribution is sufficient to publish with an honest public trail.
